name: Nimbarka
dates_notes: Sectarian dates much earlier (some traditions place him in the 11th c. or earlier); academic consensus 12th–13th c.
core_thesis:
Nimbārka's position turns on a single qualifier: *svābhāvika*. Difference (*bheda*) and non-difference (*abheda*) between Brahman, *cit*, and *acit* are both real, but they are intrinsic to the relata themselves, not produced by any limiting adjunct or by any external condition (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.42–43, on the *aṃśa-adhikaraṇa*). *Svābhāva* is the term: of-its-own-nature (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 4 *svabhāvataḥ*; *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 3.2.27). Where Bhāskara had qualified *bheda* as *aupādhika*, holding it to depend on *upādhi* and to fall away when adjuncts drop (Bhāskara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.1.14, on *upādhi-tāratamya*), Nimbārka refuses the qualification (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on 2.1.14, contrasting *svābhāvika* with *aupādhika*). The *bheda* of the *jīva* from Brahman is not adventitious; it is constitutive (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 1 *aṇuṃ hi jīvaṃ pratidehabhinnam*). The *abheda* is equally constitutive (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 1 *hareḥ adhīnam*). Neither reduces to the other (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on 2.3.42).

The *Daśa-Ślokī* and the *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* state this directly. The *jīva* is *jñāna-svarūpa*, *aṇu*, *pratidehabhinna* — atomic in measure, distinct in each body — and *hareḥ adhīna*, dependent on Hari (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 1, with the four-fold predication of v. 1ab–cd); the dependence is asymmetric, the distinctness is real, and both hold simultaneously in the *jīva*'s very nature (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.18 on *jñātā* and 2.3.19 on the *aṇu*-measure of the *jīva*).

Three reals are accordingly co-eternal: Brahman as Kṛṣṇa accompanied by Rādhā (*Daśa-Ślokī* vv. 4–5), the plural *cit* of sentient selves (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 1; *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on 2.3.42), and *acit* as insentient nature (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on 2.1.14). *Jīva* and world are *aṃśa* of Brahman in the substantial sense — dependent, controlled, non-different in essence — yet inalienably distinct as the parts of a whole that has parts of its own nature (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.43, on *aṃśo nānāvyapadeśāt*). The doxographic label *dvaitādvaita* is later usage (so the doxographies of Madhusūdana, *Prasthāna-Bheda*, and Vidyāraṇya, *Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha*); the school's own term is *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*, and the technical work is done by *svābhāvika* (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on 2.3.42).

The operative soteriology is *bhakti-prapatti* to Kṛṣṇa within the Sanaka-sampradāya (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 2 *bhagavat-prasādāt*; v. 4 *dhyāyema kṛṣṇam*). Liberation preserves the *jīva*'s real distinctness; it does not absorb the devotee into the deity (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 4.4.1–4 on the *mukta*-state). The position therefore refuses two alternatives at once: Bhāskara's *aupādhika* qualification on one side (Bhāskara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.1.14), on which difference would dissolve at liberation; and any Advaitic resolution of plurality at the limit (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 4.4.1, *avibhāga*-as-identity), on which the *jīva*'s independence would prove to have been *vyāvahārika* throughout.

engaged_works.title: Vedanta-Parijata-Saurabha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Brahma-Sūtra commentary; the Sanaka-sampradāya's school-defining bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: The *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* is Nimbārka's *Brahma-Sūtra* commentary and is among the briefest of the major *sampradāya* bhāṣyas (Bose, *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* with English translation, 1940, intro., comparing length with the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* and *Govinda-Bhāṣya*). Its compression is doctrinally deliberate. Each *sūtra* is read as licensing simultaneous and equally intrinsic *bheda* and *abheda* between Brahman and the threefold real of *cit*, *acit*, and *Īśvara* (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.42–43) — the qualifier *svābhāvika* doing the structural work that *aupādhika* did for Bhāskara (Bhāskara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 2.1.14), but in the opposite direction. The bhāṣya is identifiably Vaiṣṇava: Brahman is Kṛṣṇa with Rādhā (cf. *Daśa-Ślokī* vv. 4–5), and the soteriology is *prapatti*-inflected *bhakti* (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 3.2.24, on *bhakti* as *upāya*). Polemically the work positions itself against both *māyā-vāda* (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on 1.1.4, against Śaṅkara's *vivarta* reading of *tat tvam asi*) and Bhāskara's *aupādhika* qualification (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on 2.1.14), but the polemic is largely exegetical — by terse contrast in the construal of disputed *sūtras* — rather than by extended *pūrvapakṣa-uttarapakṣa* construction (so Bose 1940, intro., on the bhāṣya's argumentative economy). The compactness has a structural consequence. It forces the load of detailed argument onto sub-commentary, principally Śrīnivāsa's *Vedānta-Kaustubha* (on *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.1ff.), which the Sanaka-sampradāya treats as the canonical reading and which subsequent commentators (Keśava-Kāśmīrī, *Vedānta-Kaustubha-Prabhā*, sixteenth century) build on rather than going behind. The *Pārijāta-Saurabha* is the school-defining text and the obligatory reference for Nimbārka-line Vedānta (Dasgupta, *History of Indian Philosophy*, vol. III, ch. on Nimbārka).

engaged_works.title: Dasha-Shloki
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored ten-verse summary of Nimbārka's doctrine.
engaged_works.summary: The *Daśa-Ślokī* — sometimes circulated as the *Siddhānta-Ratna* — is Nimbārka's ten-verse doctrinal compendium and functions as the catechetical statement of *svābhāvika-bhedābheda* (so the printed Chowkhamba edition, 1927, with the *Laghu-Mañjūṣā* commentary). The verses do four things in tight Sanskrit. They name the three eternally real categories: *cit* as the plural sentient *jīvas* (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 1), *acit* as insentient nature (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 3), *Īśvara* as Kṛṣṇa with Rādhā (*Daśa-Ślokī* vv. 4–5). They assert the simultaneous and intrinsic *bheda* and *abheda* of *jīva* and Brahman without recourse to *upādhi* (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 1, *jñāna-svarūpaṃ ca harer adhīnam*; v. 4 *svabhāvataḥ*). They identify *bhakti* and *prapatti* as the means of liberation (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 7 on *prapatti*; cf. *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 3.2.24). And they specify the *jīva* as eternally an *aṃśa* of Brahman, never merging into and never being severed from its source (*Daśa-Ślokī* v. 1 *pratidehabhinnam*; v. 2 on the persistence of *jīva*-distinctions through bondage and liberation). Because the *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* is so terse, the *Daśa-Ślokī* carries disproportionate doctrinal weight: it is the school's portable kernel, memorised and recited, and the structural template for the later sub-commentaries by Śrīnivāsa (*Vedānta-Kaustubha*) and Keśava-Kāśmīrī (*Vedānta-Kaustubha-Prabhā*; *Krama-Dīpikā*). For readers approaching Nimbārka's settled view in his own voice, this is the most direct first-person datum available (Dasgupta, *History of Indian Philosophy*, vol. III, ch. on Nimbārka).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
jñānasvarūpaṃ ca harer adhīnaṃ
śarīrasaṃyogaviyogayogyam |
aṇuṃ hi jīvaṃ pratidehabhinnaṃ
jñātṛtvavantaṃ cānantam āhuḥ || 1 ||

pada_analysis.gloss: fit for conjunction with and disjunction from a body

pada_analysis.morphology: particle; first component of sandhi cānantam = ca + anantam
acc sg masc; second component of sandhi cānantam = ca + anantam

karaka_structure.note: Object of āhuḥ; all accusative qualifiers agree with it.

key_passages.english_close: [Its] essential form is consciousness; and that [consciousness] is dependent on Hari. It is fit for both conjunction with and disjunction from a body. The jīva, indeed, is atomic; and it is distinct in each body. They say that it is possessed of knowership and is unending [in number].
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the Daśa-Ślokī's compact definition of the jīva: dependence on Hari, atomicity, plurality, bodily embodiment, and knowership are all stated without polemical overloading. Given that the on-disk bhāṣya volumes do not expose a clean OCRable Sanskrit layer, this is the fairest securely transcribable locus for Nimbārka's ontology of the self.
key_passages.source_edition: Nimbārka, Daśa-Ślokī with Laghu-Mañjūṣā, ed. Paṇḍit Dhundhiraj Śāstrī, Chowkhamba Sanskrit Series 358 (Benares, 1927); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/nimbarka_dasasloki_archive.pdf.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
anādimāyāpariyuktarūpam
enaṃ vidur vai bhagavatprasādāt |
muktaṃ ca bhaktaṃ kila baddham uktaṃ
prabhedabāhulyam athāpi bodhyam || 2 ||

pada_analysis.gloss: whose form is associated with beginningless māyā

pada_analysis.morphology: indecl; first component of sandhi athāpi = atha + api
particle; second component of sandhi athāpi = atha + api

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: anādyā māyayā pariyuktaṃ rūpaṃ yasya tam

karaka_structure.note: Object of viduḥ; muktaṃ, bhaktaṃ, and baddham are predicated of it.
Ablative expressing causal means: knowledge arises through the Lord's grace.
Object governed by the gerundive bodhyam.

key_passages.english_close: Its form is one associated with beginningless māyā. They indeed know this [jīva] thus, by the grace of Bhagavān. It is spoken of as liberated, as devotee, and indeed as bound. And its great abundance of sub-distinctions is also to be understood.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: the beginningless power of concealment and projection)
(roughly: a devotee; here a distinct soul-condition within the taxonomy)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is representative because it does not merely state that souls exist; it states how they become knowable, through grace, and insists on enduring distinctions among liberated, devotional, and bound conditions. That combination makes it a fair doctrinal summary of Nimbārka's anti-collapsing soteriology rather than a famous isolated line.
key_passages.source_edition: Nimbārka, Daśa-Ślokī with Laghu-Mañjūṣā, ed. Paṇḍit Dhundhiraj Śāstrī, Chowkhamba Sanskrit Series 358 (Benares, 1927); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/nimbarka_dasasloki_archive.pdf.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
svabhāvato 'pāstasamastadoṣam
aśeṣakalyāṇaguṇaikarāśim |
vyūhāṅginaṃ brahma paraṃ vareṇyaṃ
dhyāyema kṛṣṇaṃ kamalekṣaṇaṃ harim || 4 ||

pada_analysis.gloss: from whom all faults are removed
the single mass of endless auspicious qualities

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: aśeṣāṇāṃ kalyāṇaguṇānām ekaḥ rāśiḥ yasya tam

karaka_structure.note: Primary object of dhyāyema; the remaining accusatives stand in apposition and describe the same object.

key_passages.english_close: From whom all faults have been removed, by his very nature. Who is the single mass of endless auspicious qualities. Who has the vyūhas as his limbs — the supreme Brahman, the most desirable. Let us meditate on him: Kṛṣṇa, the lotus-eyed Hari.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: the ordered divine emanational forms such as Vāsudeva and Saṅkarṣaṇa)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the Daśa-Ślokī's direct identification of Brahman with Kṛṣṇa and therefore an essential witness for Nimbārka's theology, not a merely liturgical embellishment. It is also where the text most compactly joins auspicious qualities, supreme status, and vyūha-theology in a single doctrinal sentence.
key_passages.source_edition: Nimbārka, Daśa-Ślokī with Laghu-Mañjūṣā, ed. Paṇḍit Dhundhiraj Śāstrī, Chowkhamba Sanskrit Series 358 (Benares, 1927); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/nimbarka_dasasloki_archive.pdf.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
aṅke tu vāme vṛṣabhānujāṃ sadā
virājamānām anurūpasaubhagām |
sakhīsahasraiḥ parisevitāṃ mudā
smaret sadā devīṃ sakaleṣṭakāmadām || 5 ||

pada_analysis.gloss: on the lap; on the side

pada_analysis.morphology: acc sg fem pres part Ā

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: sakalaṃ iṣṭaṃ kāmaṃ dadāti yā tām

karaka_structure.note: Locus of Rādhā's placement, further specified by vāme.
Object of smaret; the feminine accusative qualifiers agree with it.

key_passages.english_close: On his left side, [there is] always the daughter of Vṛṣabhānu. Resplendent there, of beauty proportionate [to him]. Attended with delight by thousands of sakhīs. One should always remember the Goddess, the granter of every desired good.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: the daughter of Vṛṣabhānu, inseparable from Kṛṣṇa in Nimbārka's theology)
(roughly: an intimate female companion in the divine retinue)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is representative because it makes Rādhā's place beside Kṛṣṇa part of the statement of the worship-worthy supreme, not a detachable later ornament. For Nimbārka's living doctrinal profile, where devotion is constitutive and not merely preparatory, that is a load-bearing claim.
key_passages.source_edition: Nimbārka, Daśa-Ślokī with Laghu-Mañjūṣā, ed. Paṇḍit Dhundhiraj Śāstrī, Chowkhamba Sanskrit Series 358 (Benares, 1927); transcribed from materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/nimbarka_dasasloki_archive.pdf.
